subject: History
title: Tribals, Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age

questions.text: Who was Birsa Munda?
questions.explanation: Birsa Munda belonged to the Munda tribe and led a movement against the outsiders and the British.

questions.text: Who were the 'Dikus' as referred to by the tribals?
questions.explanation: The term was used for people who were seen as the cause of tribal misery.

questions.text: Birsa Munda was born in which region?
questions.explanation: His movement was centered in the tribal belt of Chhotanagpur.

questions.text: What was 'Jhum' cultivation?
options: Shifting cultivation on small patches of land
questions.explanation: Cultivators cut tree tops and burnt the vegetation to clear land and enrich the soil with ash.

questions.text: Hunters and gatherers like the 'Khonds' lived in the forests of:
questions.explanation: They regularly went out on collective hunts and divided the meat among themselves.

questions.text: The tribal group 'Bakarwals' of Kashmir were:
questions.explanation: Many tribal groups were pastoralists who moved with their herds of cattle or sheep.

questions.text: The British officials saw 'settled' tribal groups like ________ as more civilized than hunters or shifting cultivators.
questions.explanation: Settled groups were easier to control and administer than mobile groups.

questions.text: Under British rule, the 'Tribal Chiefs' lost their:
questions.explanation: They were allowed to keep land titles but had to follow British laws and pay tribute.

questions.text: Why did the British want to discourage Jhum cultivation?
options: It was difficult to calculate and collect fixed revenue from mobile people
They wanted tribals to play sports
questions.explanation: The British wanted a regular revenue source and preferred settled peasants.

questions.text: Forests that produced timber which the British wanted were classified as:
questions.explanation: In reserved forests, people were not allowed to move freely or practice jhum cultivation.

questions.text: What were 'Forest Villages'?
options: Small patches of land in forests given to tribals for labor
questions.explanation: The Forest Department needed laborers to cut trees and transport logs.

questions.text: The revolt of 'Songram Sangma' in 1906 took place in:
questions.explanation: Many tribal groups reacted against the colonial forest laws.

questions.text: In the 18th century, Indian silk was in great demand in:
questions.explanation: As the demand for silk grew, the Company encouraged silk production.

questions.text: The 'Hazaribagh' region (Jharkhand) was known for the ________ who reared cocoons.
questions.explanation: The Santhals reared cocoons used to make silk.

questions.text: Why did the tribals see traders and moneylenders as their enemies?
options: They charged high interest and bought goods at very low prices
They lived in the same village
questions.explanation: Middlemen made huge profits while tribal producers earned very little.

questions.text: Many tribals were recruited to work in the ________ in Assam.
questions.explanation: They were recruited through contractors and were paid miserably low wages.

questions.text: The movement of Birsa Munda aimed at reforming ________ society.
questions.explanation: He urged Mundas to give up drinking liquor, clean their village, and stop believing in witchcraft.

questions.text: Birsa Munda talked of a 'Golden Age' or 'Satyug' when:
options: Mundas lived a good life, built embankments and practiced settled cultivation
questions.explanation: This was a vision of a past where tribals were free from the oppression of dikus.

questions.text: The political aim of the Birsa movement was to drive out missionaries, moneylenders, and the government to set up a:
questions.explanation: The movement identified all these 'dikus' as the cause of their misery.

questions.text: When did Birsa Munda die?
questions.explanation: He died of cholera in 1900, and the movement faded out.

questions.text: The British arrest of Birsa in 1895 was on charges of:
questions.explanation: He was jailed for two years after his popularity alarmed the British.

questions.text: What was the color of the flag raised by Birsa's followers as a symbol of Munda Raj?
questions.explanation: They attacked police stations and churches and raised the white flag.

questions.text: The Chhotanagpur Tenancy Act of 1908 was significant because:
options: It gave all land to the British
It made it difficult for 'dikus' to take away tribal lands
questions.explanation: The Birsa movement forced the colonial government to introduce laws to protect tribal land.

questions.text: The Baigas of Central India were a tribal group that saw themselves as:
options: People of the forest, who could only live on the produce of the forest
questions.explanation: It was below the dignity of a Baiga to become a laborer.

questions.text: The Santhals of Hazaribagh were paid ________ for a thousand cocoons which were then sold for five times that price.
questions.explanation: This shows the extreme exploitation of tribal silk rearers by middlemen.

questions.text: The 'Van Gujjars' of the Punjab hills were:
questions.explanation: They were pastoralists who moved with their animals according to the seasons.

questions.text: What was the 'Forest Satyagraha' of the 1930s?
options: A movement to plant more trees
A protest against colonial forest laws in the Central Provinces
A British plan to build parks
questions.explanation: Tribal groups across India protested against restricted access to forest resources.

questions.text: Who were the 'Gaddis' of Kulu?
questions.explanation: They were a pastoral community in the Himachal region.

questions.text: For the tribals, 'Market and Commerce' often meant:
questions.explanation: The entry of traders and moneylenders usually led to exploitation.

questions.text: What happened to tribal people who were forced to become 'settled peasants' on poor soil?
options: They often suffered as their fields did not produce good yields
questions.explanation: Settled plow cultivation was not always successful or easy in dry areas.

questions.text: Kusum and Palash flowers were used by tribals to:
questions.explanation: Local weavers and leather workers turned to the Khonds for these supplies.

questions.text: The 'Labadias' of Andhra Pradesh were ________ herders.
questions.explanation: They were among the many pastoral tribal groups in India.

questions.text: Under British rule, tribal land titles were ________ but their powers were ________.
questions.explanation: The British allowed them land ownership to keep them pacified but stripped them of administrative authority.

questions.text: The British wanted the tribals to stay in one place because:
options: It was easier to control and collect revenue
They wanted to teach them music
questions.explanation: Mobile populations are harder to tax and govern.

questions.text: Which tribal group is associated with the 'Kols' rebellion of 1831-32?
questions.explanation: Various tribal groups rebelled at different times against colonial changes.

questions.text: The 'Santhal' rebellion occurred in:
questions.explanation: The Santhals rose against the oppression of moneylenders and the British.

questions.text: The Bastar Rebellion in central India broke out in:
questions.explanation: It was one of the significant tribal uprisings in the early 20th century.

questions.text: The Warli Revolt took place in ________ in 1940.
questions.explanation: It was a movement of tribal people against the exploitation of landlords and moneylenders.

questions.text: Birsa's followers targeted which of these as a symbol of diku power?
questions.explanation: They attacked all institutions that represented foreign or exploitative authority.

questions.text: The main lesson from tribal movements like Birsa's was that tribals could:
options: Protest against injustice and colonial rule
questions.explanation: It showed their capacity to organize and challenge powerful colonial laws.
